title: Anti-Defection Law
lead: Inserted as the ==Tenth Schedule via the 52nd CA, 1985==; recalibrated by the ==91st CA, 2003==. ==CJI B.R. Gavai==: the SC, as guardian of the Constitution, is not powerless if a Speaker sits on anti-defection pleas.

items.title: Ground 1 — Voluntarily Giving Up Membership
bullets: ==Wide meaning, not defined==
[[Ravi Naik v. UoI (1994)]]: ==conduct is enough== — formal resignation unnecessary
==Halim Committee (1994)==: ‘political party’ and ‘voluntarily giving up’ should be properly defined

items.title: Ground 2 — Voting Contrary to Party Direction
bullets: ==Includes abstentions==
Forced obedience compromises free speech — but [[Kihoto Hollohan (1992)]]: a ==qualified freedom==, not in conflict with Art 19 or Arts 105/194
==170th Law Commission==: limit whips to ==confidence votes, money bills, motion of thanks==

items.title: Exemption — Merger (91st CA, 2003)
bullets: ==2/3rds merger== allowed; leftover can function as a distinct unit
Based on ==numbers, not the reason== behind defection
ECI: ==pre-poll alliances== should also count as a political unit

sections.title: The Deciding Authority — the Speaker Problem

head: Case / body
Position
rows: [[Kihoto Hollohan (1992)]]
Speaker's decision ==subject to judicial review== (post-decision); courts stay out of procedure.
[[Rajendra Singh Rana v. Swami Prasad Maurya (2007)]]

Review lies when the Speaker ==fails to act==, accepts splits/mergers without findings, or ignores the Tenth Schedule — ignoring a petition ==violates constitutional duty==. Speaker has ==no independent power== to recognise a split or merger.
[[Keisham Meghachandra Singh (2020)]]
Suggested a ==3-month timeframe==; recommended an ==independent tribunal== headed by a retired SC judge via constitutional amendment.
==Dinesh Goswami Committee (1990)== & ECI
President/Governor should decide ==on binding ECI advice==.
[[Subhash Desai (2023)]]
A mere ==‘split’ is no longer a defence==; the ==organisational party > legislative party== in dictating leadership.
[[Padi Kaushik Reddy v. Telangana (2025)]]
Speaker ==cannot indefinitely sit== on anti-defection petitions.

blocks.text: ==Outcome weakness==: disqualification carries no further penalty — not deterrent enough. Halim Committee: expelled members should be ==barred from consequential offices== and from joining other parties.

items.title: On Legislators
bullets: Undermines ==independent judgement== (public interest + constituency + party)
Deters MPs from ==holding the government accountable==
May debate and dissent — but must ==vote per the whip==

items.title: On Parliament
bullets: Decisions taken by ==a few party leaders==, not the House
Whips issued on almost everything — ==whoever controls the party controls all votes==
Winning a motion needs only ==consultation with party leaders==

items.title: On Voters
bullets: ==Breaks the chain of accountability== between representative and voter
MPs absolve themselves: ==‘the whip compelled me’==

blocks.text: ==Global rarity==: only six countries mandate voting per party diktat (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Guyana, Sierra Leone, Zimbabwe). ==US==: internal party sanctions only — [[Bond v. Floyd (1966)]]: legislators are ==obliged to take stands==. ==UK==: internal party rules, no law.

head: Episode
What happened
rows: Arunachal Pradesh (2018)
==43 Congress MLAs== led by CM Pema Khandu defected to PPA, then BJP.
Karnataka (2019)
Mass resignations → government collapse.
Manipur (2020)
SC upheld disqualification of nine MLAs.
Rajasthan (2021)
Sachin Pilot camp challenged the law in the HC.
Maharashtra (2022)
==Shinde faction (30+ MLAs)== split the MVA — claimed to be the ‘real’ party.
Telangana (2024–25)
==10 BRS MLAs== crossed to Congress — Speaker delays in proceedings.
AAP Rajya Sabha (2026)
==7 of 10 RS MPs== defected en masse, merging with BJP — organisational vs legislative party.
Tamil Nadu (2026)
==25 AIADMK rebels== voted against the whip in a confidence vote.

sections.title: Should the ADL Apply to the Rajya Sabha?

items.title: For Continuing It
bullets: Party mandate extends to both Houses — RS MPs elected ==by MLAs on party lines==
==Legislative stability== — crucial Bills need RS approval
Prevents ==horse-trading== on amendments, impeachments, committees
Preserves responsible party government (cabinet, collective responsibility, manifesto)
Nominated members' ==6-month rule== shows Parliament intended the regime to reach the RS

items.title: Against Applying It
bullets: RS is a ==deliberative chamber== — scrutiny, revision, expert debate; whips make voting mechanical
==Government survival is not at stake== — the original evil can't occur in the RS
Weakens ==federalism== — members represent States, not national party positions
==Defeats bicameralism== — no genuine second thought
Curtails free speech (Art 105); wastes the ==expertise of nominated members==

blocks.text: ADL does ==NOT apply during RS elections== by MLAs (not proceedings of the Assembly) — parties may discipline, but no Tenth Schedule disqualification. Once elected, RS MPs are ==fully governed== by it.

items: Retain disqualification for ==voluntarily giving up membership==
Restrict binding whips to ==confidence matters, Money Bills, core constitutional amendments==
Permit ==free voting on ordinary legislation== in the RS
==Statutory time limit== for the Chair/Speaker to decide petitions

blocks.text: The anti-defection law was meant to protect the mandate, not imprison the conscience of representatives. A Parliament that only counts numbers cannot become a House of wisdom.
